title: Indian AI band Trilok performs ‘live’, government denies association - Music Ally
text:
Can an AI band perform live? They can if they’re Indian “spiritual rock” quartet Trilok, which played its
debut gig at a venue in Mumbai
last week. How did the act “whose music, lyrics, and visual identity were created using AI” pull it off?
The same four masked musicians, who appear in the
clips of studio sessions shared on the band’s social media
, took the stage, after having learned its songs. Music Ally confirmed this with a PR representative for the Collective Artists Network, the talent management company that’s put together the project. Call it a reverse Milli Vanilli, if you like.
The show launched Trilok’s newest collaboration, which is with clothing brand The Souled Store for an upcoming collection of merchandise that was worn by the unidentified performers who comprise its live avatar.
The day after the concert,
the Ministry of Information & Broadcasting issued a clarification
that “Prasar Bharati has not partnered or entered into any agreement with AI generated music band named ‘Trilok’ for broadcasting content on Akashvani, Doordarshan or its OTT platform WAVES”. It further said, “A few AI-created devotional songs were telecast on the Prasar Bharati network, including the WAVES OTT platform, during Durga Navratri festival this year. This was done on a pilot basis without any monetary or recurring commitment.”
The statement followed increasing discussions in Parliament about the use of artificial intelligence in state-funded media. Curiously, the
press release shared by the Collective Artists Network announcing the alleged partnership
with Prasar Bharati—which was shared in the third week of October after the end of the Durga Navratri festival—quoted the public service broadcaster’s CEO as saying “Partnering with Collective Media Network allows us to showcase how technology and storytelling can come together in ways that inspire, entertain, and connect communities.”
Trilok has released five tracks, including two “live” versions, in the interim. As it feels obligatory, we’re sharing an updated count for their monthly listeners on Spotify, which now stand at 2,131. That’s almost double the 1,135 figure they had around the time of the Prasar Bharati announcement, but not quite a number that makes the act seem like a serious threat to their human counterparts.
